item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Two awesome dino SCPs posted right next to each other. Love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was good. Like really good. Made the dino nerd in me happy. And I know this was made years ago but hey, it's never too late to say stuff right. Now that I've read this though I'm going to go back and carefully look at the names of people, to see if there are any references :).

Also someone asked a similair question to this five years ago but they didn't get an answer. Is Dr Neill a reference to Sam Neill.
(For those who don't know Sam Neill played Alan Grant in the Jurassic park trilogy, and soon insert happy nerd noises Jurassic world trilogy.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really really like this post, but I do have a minor corrections- your article features a quadrupedal spinosaurus, and while it was theorized for a while that the animal walked on all fours, this idea is now highly scrutinized by scientists.
Nevertheless, I really enjoyed this SCP. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fun Valley of the Dinosaurs vibe and detailed descriptions make this a +1 from me. The idea that some group or organisation is running things behind the scenes and may be linked to the dino farm just makes it even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very good scip, though there's one thing that irks the paleo-nerd inside me.
The fact that every single dinosaur in this scp has feathers, despite there being evidence IRL contradicting it. There are a good number of dinosaurs that clearly didn't have them. Feathers would be useless for sauropods, large ornithopods, as well as many other megafaunal taxa because most of them exceed the 3 ton rule. Many of them would lose feathery integument for the same reasons that elephants lost hair. In fact, there are even some theropods that show signs of a purely scaly integument such as Carnotaurus, as well as a good amount of other taxa with skin impressions showing nothing but scales. Sauropods, hadrosaurs, and stegosaurs were completely scaly as far as we know. Feathers would be present in smaller, medium-sized to intermediate-sized taxa, such as basal neoornithischians, heterodontosaurids, and most definitely maniraptorans and other coelurosaurs, and modern, fully developed birdlike feathers were exclusive to maniraptorans. Non-maniraptoran coelurosaurs would have had just downy, hairlike feathers. Basal neoornithischians had something similar, but theirs grew off of scale-like plates. Heterodontosaurids were seemingly similar in that regard, but with the addition of quills.

Yes, I know this is science fiction, but the thing is that this scip is trying to representing scientifically accurate versions of dinosaurs. Putting feathers on a sauropod isn't scientifically accurate, it is a paradox. At least to me, anyway.

But with that out of the way, I think this is a very interesting SCP. Despite the scientific errors, this one really captured the imagination. The bit with the strange abnormal ceratopsian was especially interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe no Paleontologists, but definitely ones going to school for it (Pssst, It's me)!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Hot damn!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I have absolutely no problems with this.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

The fact that SCP-346 exists suggests the possibility that similar anomalies to SCP-1265 may lurk elsewhere in the wilds of the planet. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1265 is believed to have existed for at least 200 years, according to historical records. Several instances of SCP-1265-A have been integrated into the folklore of several tribes in the area near SCP-1265. For further details, see Interview 1265-01.

To this:

SCP-1265 is believed to have existed for at least 200 years, according to historical records. Several instances of SCP-1265-A have been integrated into the folklore of several tribes in the area near SCP-12651. For further details, see Interview 1265-01.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Objects such as SCP-346 suggest anomalies similar to SCP-1265 may exist elsewhere, though no additional instances have been found.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Three years, and nobody noticed that the Coelophysis broke "trhee" of it's ankles? I feex :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so glad that there are no actual paleontologists reading the wiki, or else I'd have been crucified long ago. I just corrected several greviousl paleontological errors I made previously, and added some new bits in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
<creepy nerd breathing>

Useless nitpick, and I'm not sure if it was intended, but the dilophosaurus in the certain movie doesn't act like a dog at all during the scene in question - it ignores the poor bastard's stick to follow him around some more.

</creepy nerd breathing>

Otherwise, I'm a big fan of this - Lost World adventuring, cryptozoology, and dinosaurs all in one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In footnote 2, is the text supposed to be "α" instead of "A"? Because it doesn't make sense that someone would specifically attribute violent behavior to a nonviolent dinosaur when violent ones exist.

Additionally, the use of "A" and "α" is, while unimportant in this particular SCP, potentially subject to confusion. This is just me, but I would change it to "A" and "B". It's not really important here, but for an SCP that, say, produces creatures which have to be doused with acid if blue and doused with a base if red, and ABSOLUTELY NOT VICE VERSA; someone frantically glancing through the documentation would have a slightly easier time of it. That said, from the perspective of a reader, the use of corresponding greek and regular letters reinforces the idea that the "α" class is only a slight variation on the "A" class, and will eventually change. So I suppose I could go either way.

I've got no complaints regarding not applying amnestics to the natives, but you may want to mention the creation of a disinformation campaign meant to ensure any tales one of them tells to an outsider is seen as a myth. For example, creating interest in the myths of the tribes of the area to build the image of them to the outside world as superstitious, leading to any odd statements from them being taken as fairy tales. Or the planting of slightly or completely false stories about the area within the tribe, as well as reinforcing the idea that it is dangerous so people don't go there so the false stories spread and aren't disproved. Or both. Additionally, the Foundation has area-effect amnestics, so a large group of people knowing about the SCP isn't really a reason not to make them forget about it. Some possible reasons could include the people's remoteness, reclusivity, and very rare language.
Also, I would recommend inserting a reference to a document containing a full listing of both known and unknown dinosaurs sighted - it seems like the sort of thing the Foundation would have.

Minor complaints aside, this is an excellent article and I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hope I'm not necroposting…

"the 3 Foundation personnel killed in SCP-1265 have been killed…" the double kill doesn't sounds right to me.

Notably, there are few large carnivorous or herbivorous dinosaurs present as SCP-1265-A instances, and a lack of dinosaur species whose fossils have been found in North America. For example, species such as Tyrannosaurus rex and Brachiosaurus have never been sighted in SCP-1265, but species such as Spinosaurus and Kentrosaurus are relatively common. Proportionately speaking, there is a 5:1 ratio of non-American dinosaurs to American species.

Why is this relevant? Also, considering there are six continents (counting America as a single continent) that means that either each continent except for one (Antarctica?) has the same ratio or the total abscence of dinosaurs from other continents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awesome! +1
And I just wanted to say that it's comment sections like this one that keep me reading comments, and that show just how friggin wide and deep the knowledge base of our contributors is. Not sucking up, just pleased to see biological, grammatical, paleontological, and ethnographic/linguistic fact checking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, although they have a short life-span (around 3/4 years in captivity), rats are quite smart, definitely smarter than a dog.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this article. Minor irks as mentioned by others (regarding sloppy cryptozoology) but unrelated to the piece. Well done. Last thing: Dr. Neill's first name wouldn't be Sam, would it?

item.title: Re: +100. Need I say anything else?
item.content:encoded:
eeeee

EEEE

EEEEEEEEE

Thankyouthankyouthankyou! All of you! Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My inner six-year old, dinosaur loving self is very, very pleased.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We could put it in the discussion :c

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neither does Mokele before people started pushing natives to alter their descriptions :P but hey, why not XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Re: Dilophosaurus — if this is a Jurassic Park reference, which I'm assuming it is, I'm sort of confused by the dog reference

That was just the Doctor mis-remembering the movie, where Nedry (the guy that got killed by the Dilophosaurus) was trying to play with it like it was a dog and make it play fetch.

This isn't critique, but the only reference I didn't get was the last one. Out of curiosity, what was that … thing?

It's a creature similar to Mokele-mbembe, called Emela-ntouka, a supposedly ceratopisan creature that lives in the same area as Mokele. It doesn't match the description of any ceratopsid ever discovered, but I wanted to include it just for kicks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted this because I feel like honestly how can I not. The choice of picture is brilliant and I love all the dinosaur discussion nonsense.

But now for critique. And by the way, I understand that most of these are specialized complaints, but hey.

  • The whole "Mokele-mbembe == omg teh dinosars" thing is a bunch of bullshit spread by sloppy crypotozoologists (more recently motivated by religious reasons). Seeing it here breaks my suspension of disbelief by quite a bit. [Though I'm not really sure what's to be done about that, it's not bothering anyone else & is a big part of the article.]
  • Depiction of Kentrosaurus is a bit unlikely, given how stegosaur diet likely actually worked, but honestly, it was creative enough that I enjoyed it nonetheless and likely wouldn't change it.
  • "Despite the theory created by Mr. Russell in the 1980s, there is absolutely no evidence that Troodon would ever have evolved into the so-called "dinosauroid"" > This also feels forced in as a fun reference for dinosaur nerds. I'm torn, because it is fun, but I can't really believe that this scientist would've made any note about it whatsoever in Foundation documents, even if they actually had a bug up their butt about what was always just a fun bit of speculation.
  • Re: Dilophosaurus — if this is a Jurassic Park reference, which I'm assuming it is, I'm sort of confused by the dog reference, given that JP altered the animal to be a combination of frilled lizard and spitting cobra. I don't recall doglike behavior :P

This isn't critique, but the only reference I didn't get was the last one. Out of curiosity, what was that … thing?

I really liked the depiction of the therizinosaur, and putting the baryonyx in a family unit, and the microraptor thing was fun, as was the choice to set it in the past and have surprise about the feathers thing.

(Though it strikes me as curious — perhaps unnecessarily — that the researchers didn't differentiate between the literal feathers of the theropods and the pseudo-fur/feathery structures of the ornithopods & marginocephalians; also, were the kentrosaurs meant to be feathered too? they're listed before postosuchus, but I'm not aware of thyreophorans having feather-like structures, though given that before recently no one guessed that for ornithopds & marginocephalians, it doesn't break my suspension of disbelief!)

Also as another side note — out of curiosity, have you read All Yesterdays? I ask because some of the cool speculative work you did here reminded me of it =]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Look, honestly: In my opinion, we should all get used to that sort of thing. This is the way we don't be shitty. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, c'mon, is that link under the pic really necessary?

item.title: Image is back up!
item.content:encoded: Image "Velociraptor portrait 2" is © Alain Bénéteau - all right reserved.

item.title: Image Removed because the artist never got into contact with me. >_>
item.content:encoded: Considering it has literally been five months and no response from the artist at all despite three separate attempts to contact him, I've removed the image for this article, considering that the image team will get to it eventually. If you really want to see the picture, there are a couple of links in the thread.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
In accordance with the updated image use policy, I have contacted Alain Beneteau (AKA dustdevil) on deviantart and properly asked for permission to use his "Velociraptor portrait 2", which is currently the page image. I am currently awaiting word back from him on whether I can continue to use this image, but until that time, I'll keep the image up so that he can see it within the context of the SCP. If he responds in the negative, I'll just take down the image, but in case he responds in the positive, I'll put an image credit for him here now:

This image is borrowed from:

Original Source:http://dustdevil.deviantart.com/art/Velociraptor-portrait-2-67697051
Creator: Alain Beneteau (Dustdevil)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely loved this. Un'goro Crater, but with some hint of something extremely weird going on regarding the docility of the animals…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is delicious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very cool, a lost world in the Congo as an SCP is a brilliant idea.

One question though, it says that all the Dinosaurs were coated in feathers, does that even include the Sauropods? I find it odd that something like a Brachiosaur or a Stegosaur would have feathers coating its entire body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the one part about this that bugs me: Russel's Dinosaurid theory was based on the fact that Troodons might have had approximately the same level of intelligence as the mammals that lived at the time, meaning that either group may have been equally likely to advance to human-like intelligence, millions of years down the line. However, considering that the mammals of the time were basically things like rats and voles, and probably not even as smart as those, this is not saying much at all. So even though Troodon was very intelligent for a dinosaur, it still would probably make your average chicken look like a genius, much less have chimp level brainpower. So yeah, the tool thing sorta bugs me. The rest of the article, though, I love.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My inner dinosaur nerd is pleased. All I ask for are more exploration logs. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might be helpful to point out specifics here, in this case. Or you can PM the author if you feel thus inclined.

If you notice minor grammatical errors in articles, you're free to edit accordingly, so long as you leave a description of the changes you've made (via the edit screen) and the changes are not substantial (if you're ever unsure about an edit you'd like to make, contact a staff member).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fix the last 2 or three spellings before i upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The bird-thing is grinning at me, and I would like it to stop, please.

I really want to know more about what's going on behind the scenes. Who or what is collecting these samples and herding them to stay in the boundary? Why do they have this bias against North America? And what is up with the agression of the undiscovered? Are they upset that they aren't getting the recognition they deserve?

Auto-upvote for making me curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shit, you're right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no such thing as a brachiosaurus. It came from a misidentified apatosaurus skeleton.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Impressive! I've read up a bit on Mblieu and those other things, so I saw what you did there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how this scp ended up turning out; the logs and the cultural connections are very engaging. I'd love it if the logs didn't focus so heavily on the dinos killing each other, but very well done.

- Hide

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only a cookie? Have an entire damn internet!

+ show block
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good SCP. I enjoyed reading it, and now I'm enjoying trying to guess the origin of the image. A quick search using Google Images turns up an identical image on a dinosaur game forum, which was sourced from Deviantart, leading me to an artist named "dustdevil". Dustdevil appears to be an enthusiast of paleontology and nature art. After sorting through his/her gallery, I found the original post of the image. There's not much info from the artist himself/herself, but my guess as well as some of the commentators is that the image is a photo manipulation of a bearded vulture, a kind of European vulture that strongly resembles prehistoric raptors. All Dustdevil would have to do is replace the beak with a snout and add the teeth. In fact, upon close examination it becomes clear that the snout and teeth have been drawn on using what appears to be either colored pencil or pastels with such realism and precision that the fact is not immediately obvious unless the image is examined under high quality and close magnification.

Do I win a cookie?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see I've missed some good articles in my absence. Really, though, this is cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So many awesome implications in this. I particularly like the idea that a species being discovered elsewhere causes its representatives here to become docile.

One thing that caught my attention is the word "chosen" in the early parts of the description. Who or what is doing the choosing, and how do researchers know about it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last one is an Emela-Ntouka, isn't it?

This gives the junior-cryptozoologist-child part of my brain very fuzzy and nostalgic feelings, and the article is good, so take an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, love it. The only thing that bugs me is the a into A transition. Why us it if we have not discovered the fossils they are aggressive? Are they mad because we never found them? Find and name the fossils they are suddenly happy? Don't get that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had to handle some its/it's errors, but otherwise this looks decent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feex a spelling thing. Looks good!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did not do the research, clearly. X_X

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upboated because this is super good, honestly, but two ethnographic corrections:

Several instances of SCP-1265-A have been integrated into the folklore of the Lingala and Baka(pygmy) tribes in the area near SCP-1265

Lingala is the language, not the speakers of the language; there is no Lingala tribe or people. Baka and pygmy are not the same; the Baka are an ethnic group whose name has historically been used inaccurately to describe the entire group of bambenga and bayaka peoples in central Africa and the Congo. The term "pygmy" is generally considered by the people to whom the term refers to be perjorative.

An interview conducted by Dr. Welles in a Lingala village 5.5 KM away from the perimeter of SCP-1265 regarding SCP-1265-A sightings. All dialog is translated from the Lingala Language.

Again, there is no Lingala tribe, and thus no Lingala villages. The village is probably an Mboshi one, if the reserve is where I think it is. Also, language doesn't need to be capitalized.

There may be more, but these are the ones that jumped out at me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, I love this idea.

Second of all, good job on remembering the italics for genus names and the like. There are some places where you shouldn't use italics - as a clade name, Dinosauria remains unitalicized; and if you're talking about a taxonomic group of animals (e.g. spinosaur) the name of it is generally unitalicized and lowercase as well. (A couple spelling mistakes regarding names as well - for example Archeopteryx > Archaeopteryx and Celophysis > Coelophysis. And Yinxin Formation should be spelled as Yixian…I don't blame you though, I have a hard time wrapping my head around the Chinese language too.) There are a couple of other details that strike me as well, although I can't put my finger on it exactly. All this does seem a bit nitpicky, but paleontology is my thing so I can't help myself :P

Third, I must say the imagery of Therizinosaurus is quite delightful, especially in the context you've given it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Grammar, what are you doing?

Grammar.

STAHP.

(seriously, though, I fix.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
instead, they appear to be selected based on they climate they lived in in the Mesozoic Era

You say the word "in" twice. IMO, the word "during" should replace the second "in." Other than that, I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What 1XXX's? I most definitely didn't forget to put change them because I am almost half-asleep and I rushed to get this article posted this morning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Need to fix your SCP-1XXX's
